nativeRange.habitat: Found on shallow coral reefs and in tide pools, typically between 0 and 50 m depth. This species is well known for its ability to move across the reef by 'walking' on its paired pectoral and pelvic fins.

diet.notes: Has been documented consuming benthic invertebrates in the wild including polychaete worms, small crabs, shrimp, amphipods, and small fish. In captivity, generally accepted offerings include vitamin-enriched squid, shrimp, mussels, clams, and pieces of fresh marine fish. Feeding 2–3 times per week with vitamin supplementation is commonly recommended.

goodWith: large peaceful fish that cannot be swallowed
large non-aggressive species housed with caution
avoidWith: fish smaller than the shark's mouth
large angelfish that may harass shark

summary: The Australian Epaulette Shark is a bottom-dwelling reef shark found along the coasts of Australia and Papua New Guinea, known for its ability to walk across the reef using its paired fins. It is generally advised only for expert aquarists due to its large adult size and demanding space requirements.
careNotes:
The Australian Epaulette Shark is a highly specialized species generally advised only for expert aquarists with very large, dedicated systems. Adults have been reported to reach up to 107 cm (42 in) in total length, though aquarium-kept specimens commonly reach 70–90 cm. A minimum tank footprint where the tank length is at least 3× the shark's body length and the tank depth equals the shark's length is commonly recommended; in practice this means tanks of 1,000–1,400 L (approximately 260–360 US gallons) are the working minimum.

Sand substrate is essential — the shark's abdomen is prone to abrasion injuries from coarse gravel, which can lead to secondary infection. Caves, overhangs, and sheltered hiding areas are important for this nocturnal species, which tends to rest during the day and become active at dusk.

The Australian Epaulette Shark is a nocturnal ambush predator and will consume any fish, shrimp, or crab small enough to fit in its mouth. It is not considered reef-safe due to predation on small invertebrates and sleeping fish. Copper-based medications must be strictly avoided, as is the case with all elasmobranchs (cartilaginous fish, including sharks and rays), which are broadly understood to be highly sensitive to copper.

The species is notable for its physiological tolerance of low-oxygen and high-temperature conditions in tidal pools — an adaptation that has attracted scientific study. Despite this resilience in the wild, captive specimens still require stable, high-quality water.

Lifespan is reported variously as 8–12 years and up to 25+ years in well-managed captive environments; the wide range reflects limited published data.

The species is oviparous (egg-laying). Captive breeding has been documented at professional aquaculture facilities, with eggs reported to hatch after approximately 130 days. Captive breeding in home aquaria has been reported but remains uncommon, and is generally considered to require a very large, low-stress system.
breedingNotes: The Australian Epaulette Shark is oviparous, meaning it lays eggs rather than giving birth to live young. Paired eggs are laid and embryos develop solely on yolk, with no parental involvement after laying. Eggs are reported to measure approximately 10 × 4 cm and hatch after roughly 130 days at suitable temperatures. Successful captive breeding has been documented at professional aquaculture facilities, with pairs typically requiring several years to reach sexual maturity. Captive breeding in home aquaria has been reported but remains uncommon; a large, stable, low-stress environment is generally considered a prerequisite.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; eggs hatch after approximately 130 days. Captive breeding has been documented at professional facilities but remains uncommon in home aquaria. A large, dedicated system with stable water quality is generally considered a prerequisite.
